records.ety: [F. dragon dragon, dragoon, fr. L. draco dragon, also, a cohort's standard (with a dragon on it). The name was given from the sense standard. See Dragon.]

defs: Formerly, a soldier who was taught and armed to serve either on horseback or on foot; now, a mounted soldier; a cavalry man.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dragooned (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Dragooning.]

defs: To harass or reduce to subjection by dragoons; to persecute by abandoning a place to the rage of soldiers.
To compel submission by violent measures; to harass; to persecute.
quotes: The colonies may be influenced to anything, but they can be dragooned to nothing.
Lewis the Fourteenth is justly censured for trying to dragoon his subjects to heaven.
